description: Delhi High Court commercial litigation focus. Indian federal commercial, taxation, and insolvency law.

questions.question: Under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, what is the limitation period for filing a complaint, and how did the Delhi High Court clarify the cause-of-action accrual in 2023?
questions.goldenAnswer: Under Section 142(b) of the NI Act, a complaint must be filed within one month of the cause of action under Section 138(c) — the date on which the 15-day payment window after demand notice expires. Delhi HC in Umesh Bhargava v. Vijay Shankar Pathak (2023) reaffirmed that each returned cheque gives rise to an independent cause of action and that filing delay beyond one month is fatal absent condonation under the proviso to Section 142(b).

questions.question: What are the practical implications of the Specific Relief (Amendment) Act 2018 for parties seeking specific performance of construction contracts in India?
questions.goldenAnswer: The Specific Relief (Amendment) Act 2018 amended Section 10 to make specific performance the rule (not discretionary) for breach of contract, removing the court's discretion except in limited circumstances. For construction contracts, the amendment enables courts to engage qualified experts and technical supervisors to oversee contract performance. The proviso preserves discretion where specific performance would involve performance of a continuous duty requiring constant supervision, as in complex infrastructure contracts.

questions.question: Under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code 2016, what is the minimum threshold for filing a Section 9 operational creditor petition, and what procedural preconditions must be satisfied?
questions.goldenAnswer: Under Section 9 of the IBC, an operational creditor may initiate CIRP if the operational debt exceeds Rs. 1 crore (raised from Rs. 1 lakh by the 2020 amendment). Preconditions: (1) a demand notice must be delivered under Section 8; (2) the corporate debtor must fail to repay the debt or dispute it within 10 days; (3) the creditor must confirm no notice of dispute has been received. The NCLT applies CoC of India Ltd v. Amit Metaliks (2021) to assess pre-existing disputes.

questions.question: The Finance Act 2023 abolished angel tax under Section 56(2)(viib) of the Income Tax Act for foreign investors. What is the current legal position and its impact on startup funding rounds?
questions.goldenAnswer: The Finance Act 2023 exempted foreign investors from the Section 56(2)(viib) angel tax provisions. The Finance Act 2024 further abolished Section 56(2)(viib) entirely (effective April 1, 2025), removing the deemed income tax on excess consideration over fair market value for all investors. Startups recognised by DPIIT were already exempt. The abolition removes a major friction from Series A/B funding rounds in India and eliminates tax litigation risk on preference share premiums.

questions.question: Under Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act, what is the enforceability of contractual clauses that restrict parties from initiating legal proceedings beyond a specified time?
questions.goldenAnswer: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act makes void any agreement that absolutely restricts a party from enforcing their rights in court. However, Exception 1 permits agreements to enforce rights only in a specified court. Exception 2 permits arbitration clauses. The Supreme Court in BSNL v. Subash Chandra Kanchan (2006) held that agreed time limits shorter than the statutory limitation period are void as restraints on legal proceedings, unless falling within the exceptions.
